1: 'The wilderness and the dry land will rejoice; the desert will exult and blossom like the crocus' — the opening of the great reversal-chapter: 'yesussum midbar vetsiyah, the wilderness and the dry land will rejoice.' The midbar (wilderness/desert = the unproductive, uninhabitable zone) and tsiyah (the parched/dry land) personified as rejoicing communities. The wilderness rejoices: the same root (sus = to exult) as the Deuteronomic blessing (Deuteronomy 28:63: 'just as it pleased the LORD to make you prosper and increase in number, so it will please him to ruin and destroy you') here reversed — YHWH takes pleasure in the wilderness-restoration. 'Blossom like the crocus' (veti-chach kachabatseleth, and blossom like the crocus/rose of Sharon) — the chabatseleth (the crocus or narcissus or autumn crocus) = the first flower of spring appearing in the desert after rain. Contrast with Isaiah 34's desolation and with Isaiah 33:9's withering landscapes.
2: 'It will blossom with lavish abundance and rejoice with joy and singing. The glory of Lebanon will be given to it, the splendor of Carmel and Sharon; they will see the glory of the LORD, the majesty of our God' — the restoration of the withered landscapes (Isaiah 33:9): 'paroch tiffrach vegil, it will blossom fully and rejoice.' The three great landscape-symbols of fertility (33:9 had them withering): Lebanon (the cedar-forest glory), Carmel (the productive mountain-garden), Sharon (the coastal agricultural plain) — their kavod (glory) and hadar (splendor) transferred to the formerly barren desert. 'They will see the glory of the LORD' (hemmah yiru kevod YHWH, they — the people going through the restored wilderness — will see the glory of YHWH) and 'the majesty of our God' (hadar Elohenu, the splendor of our God). The landscape-glory is YHWH's glory manifested in creation's restoration.
3: 'Strengthen the weak hands, and steady the shaking knees' — the command to encourage the discouraged community: 'chazzequu yadayim rafot, strengthen the weak hands.' The rafot yadaim (slack/hanging hands = the physical gesture of despair and defeat, 2 Samuel 4:1; Ezekiel 7:17; Zephaniah 3:16) to be 'strengthened' (chazaq = to be strong, the same chazaq as YHWH's 'be strong and courageous' commands to Joshua in Deuteronomy 31:6-7, 23). 'Steady the shaking knees' (uvirkayim koshelot ammetsu, and the stumbling/tottering knees, make firm) — the knees that tremble in fear (Nahum 2:10; Daniel 5:6: Belshazzar's knees knocked together) are commanded to be made firm. The command is to the remaining faithful community: they are to encourage the discouraged, not merely await YHWH's action passively.
4: 'Say to those whose hearts are gripped with fear: Be strong; do not be afraid! See — your God will come with vengeance, with divine recompense. He will come and save you' — the herald's announcement: 'imru lenimhare-lev chizqu al-tira'u, say to those who are fearful-of-heart: be strong, do not fear.' The nihmere-lev (the anxious/hasty-of-heart = those whose hearts are in a state of panicked rush) addressed with the covenant comfort-formula 'do not be afraid' (al-tira'u) that appears throughout the patriarchal narratives (Genesis 15:1; Isaiah 26:24; Isaiah 46:3) and Isaiah (Isaiah 7:4; Isaiah 40:9; Isaiah 41:10-14; Isaiah 43:1-5). 'Your God will come with vengeance' (hineh Eloheikhem, naqam yavo, behold your God — vengeance will come) and 'divine recompense' (gemul Elohim = the recompense/retribution of God). 'He will come and save you' (hu yavo veyoshi'akhem, he will come and save you) — the definitive salvific coming.
5: 'Then the eyes of the blind will be opened, and the ears of the deaf will be unstopped' — the four physical-healing signs of the messianic era: 'az tippaqachna einei ivre'im, then will be opened the eyes of the blind.' The tippaqachna (will be opened = from paqach = to open the eyes, specifically used for the opening of blind eyes, Genesis 21:19: God 'opened her eyes' at Hagar's crisis; 2 Kings 6:17-20: Elisha praying 'open his eyes'). 'The ears of the deaf will be unstopped' (ve'ozne cherashim tipatachnah, and the ears of the deaf will be opened) — patach (to open) applied to ears. These healings reverse the sealing/closing of Isaiah 29:10-12 (spirit of deep sleep, eyes shut, ears covered) and Isaiah 6:9-10 (the people who hear but do not understand, see but do not perceive). Jesus cites this exact verse (with Isaiah 35:6) in Matthew 11:5 as proof of messianic presence to John the Baptist's disciples.
6: 'Then the lame will leap like a deer, and the tongue of the mute will burst into song. For waters will break out in the wilderness and streams flow through the desert' — the healing extending to limbs and speech: 'az yedallleg kaayyal pisseyach, then the lame will leap like a deer.' The pisseyach (lame/limping = from passach, related to Passover, from the 'limping' at the threshold; 1 Kings 18:21: Elijah's challenge about 'limping between two opinions'). The deer-leap (dallleg from dalag = to leap/spring, Song of Solomon 2:8: 'leaping across the mountains, bounding over the hills') = the most agile, joyful movement. 'The tongue of the mute will burst into song' (velashon illem terannen, and the tongue of the mute will shout for joy) — the illem (the speechless/mute, from alam = to bind/silence). Waters in the wilderness (Isaiah 35:6b-7): the dual healing of human bodies and the physical landscape. Acts 8:39 (the Ethiopian eunuch going on his way rejoicing) and Acts 3:8 (the lame man leaping) fulfill the specific imagery.
7: 'The burning sand will become a pool, and the thirsty ground springs of water; in the haunt of jackals, where they once rested, the grass will grow with reeds and rushes' — the ecological transformation of the desert: 'vehayah hashharav le'agam, and the burning sand will become a lake/pool.' The sharav (the mirage/burning sand of the desert = the shimmering heat-haze that looks like water but is not, the deceptive mirage) becomes an actual agam (a standing lake). 'The thirsty ground springs of water' (vetsimaon lemabbu'e mayim, and the thirsty/parched ground springs of water) = the parched cracked earth of the desert floor becomes a spring-source. 'In the haunt of jackals, where they once rested, the grass will grow with reeds and rushes' (binveh tannim ravtsah, in the dwelling of jackals, their resting-place, will be grass with reed and rush) — the Isaiah 34:13's 'haunt of jackals' (the desolate ruin) is now transformed into a wetland where grass, reeds, and rushes grow.
8: 'And a highway will be there, called the Way of Holiness; the unclean will not travel over it. It is for those who walk the road; even fools will not lose their way on it' — the Way of Holiness: 'vehayah sham maslul vaderekh, and a highway will be there, and a road.' The maslul (raised highway/paved road, the same root as mesillah = the main road) = a public infrastructure project in the desert. 'Called the Way of Holiness' (derekh haqodesh yiqqare' lah, the Way of Holiness will it be called) — qodesh (holiness) defines the road: not merely a physical highway but a sacred processional route. 'The unclean will not travel over it' (lo ya'averennah tame, the unclean will not pass over it) — the purity requirement for travel. 'Even fools will not lose their way on it' (ve'eviliym lo yit'u, and fools will not wander) — the Way of Holiness is so clearly marked that even the spiritually unsophisticated can follow it without getting lost. The Way theology anticipates John 14:6 ('I am the way').
9: 'No lion will be there, nor will any ravenous beast be found on it. But the redeemed will walk there' — the safety of the Way of Holiness: 'lo-yihyeh sham aryeh, no lion will be there.' The lion (aryeh) = the apex predator of the ANE wilderness, the danger that made travel on ancient roads hazardous. 'Nor will any ravenous beast be found on it' (vepharits chayot bal-ya'alennah, and no savage wild animal will ascend onto it) — the predator-free sacred road. The contrasting population: 'vehalekhu gealim, but the redeemed will walk' (gealim = the redeemed = those YHWH has redeemed/bought back, from ga'al = the kinsman-redeemer who buys back a family member from slavery: Leviticus 25:25-54). The redeemed walking where lions dare not tread = the total reversal of wilderness danger. Isaiah 11:6-9 describes the peaceable kingdom where the lion lies down with the lamb.
10: 'The ransomed of the LORD will return; they will come to Zion with singing, with everlasting joy on their heads. They will reach gladness and joy, and sorrow and sighing will flee away' — the climax of the restoration: 'ufeduyey YHWH yeshuevun, and the ransomed of YHWH will return.' The feduyey (ransomed = from padah = to redeem by payment, the commercial redemption that pays the price, vs. ga'al = the family redemption of Isaiah 35:9). The two redemption words together: gealim (Isaiah 35:9, family redemption) and feduyey (Isaiah 35:10, purchase redemption) = the comprehensive redemption language. 'They will come to Zion with singing' (uvau Tsiyon berinah, and they will come to Zion with ringing joy) — the processional arrival at Zion of the exiles returning. 'Everlasting joy on their heads' (simchat olam al-rosham, everlasting joy upon their heads) = the festive garland of joy replacing the ash of mourning (Isaiah 61:3). 'Sorrow and sighing will flee away' (venassu yagon va'anachah, and sorrow and sighing will flee) — quoted in Revelation 21:4 alongside the 'no more death, mourning, crying, pain.'
